attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# jjezs_paddock ₣BR

## Shape

This heat hardens RBK against GCP behaviors that surface only under real-world churn. Current content: one problem — **depot projectId reservation collisions**.

GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after a project is deleted (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker allocator picks "next free" by walking a same-identity displayName search (`projects:search`, via depot_list). That search is identity-scoped: it returns the caller's own projects including their DELETE_REQUESTED ones — so **same-identity churn self-heals**, the allocator increments past its own pending-delete depots.

The blindness is **cross-identity**: a projectId reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project is invisible to the caller's search. The allocator then derives a moniker whose global ID is already held elsewhere, and project creation fails with 409. This bit the project when the payor switched from the gmail (no-org) identity to the scaleinvariant identity: gmail's deleted `canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to the scaleinvariant allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor.

## Scope and urgency

Low urgency. Same-identity churn already self-heals; the residual collision recurs **only on a payor-identity switch** — rare. The session that found it worked around it with a family-stem bump. This is robustness, not a blocker.

## Undecided (design, not yet settled)

- **Where the fix lives.** The collider was the *test fixture* moniker picker (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, Rust); the production levy (`rbgp_depot_levy`) does not auto-pick — it uses the configured `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` and 409s on collision. So "fixture-only Rust fix" vs "production levy change (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`/`RBSDN`)" is an open choice — different surfaces, different blast radius.
- **What the fix does.** A candidate-ID availability probe (GET → 404 free / 403 owned-elsewhere / 200 own; increment past taken) tests the real global constraint instead of the same-identity proxy — versus a lighter "clearer 409 + operator guidance" approach.

## Constraints

- Any bash work here reads **BCG** (Bash Console Guide) first. `rbgp_Payor.sh` is a complex BCG-compliant module; do not write bash against it without the guide.

## What done looks like

A decision on fix surface (fixture vs production) and fix shape (probe vs guidance), then the implementation plus matching spec update. Until then, paces stay uncut.

content.text:
Groomed. Here's where ₣BR stands.

## ₣BR — rbk-16-gcp-hardening
**Status:** stabled · **Created:** 260521 · **Paces:** none cut yet

The paddock holds exactly one problem, well-characterized, and it explicitly gates pace-cutting on two undecided design questions:

- **Where the fix lives** — fixture-only Rust (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`) vs production levy (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`/`RBSDN`)
- **What the fix does** — candidate-ID availability probe (GET → 404/403/200) vs lighter "clearer 409 + operator guidance"

The closing line is `Until then, paces stay uncut.` So the groom question isn't "what paces" — it's "are these two decisions ready to make."

## Musing — is it time?

My read: **the two axes aren't independent, and once you see that, the decision is most of the way made.**

Look at what each surface actually does:

- **Production levy already fails loud.** It uses the configured `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` and 409s on collision. There's a human in the loop who *chose* that moniker. A probe there only pre-empts a 409 with a nicer message — marginal. What production wants, if anything, is **guidance**.
- **The fixture auto-picks and collides silently.** That's where the actual bug lives — "next free" walks a same-identity search and derives a globally-taken ID. There's no operator to guide. What the fixture wants is a **probe** that makes auto-pick correct against the real global constraint.

So the cross-product collapses to two coherent packages, not four:
- Fixture → probe (auto-pick must be correct cross-identity)
- Production → clearer 409 + guidance (already loud, just sharper)

And they're not mutually exclusive — you could want both. But they're very different in weight: the fixture probe is the thing that actually self-heals test churn across an identity switch; the production guidance is a one-paragraph error-message + spec touch.

Given the paddock's own "low urgency, robustness not blocker" framing, here's the readiness question I'd put to you:

1. **Is the fixture probe alone sufficient?** It's the only surface with a silent-failure bug. If yes, this is a *one-pace* heat (probe + matching `RBSDE`/`RBSDN` spec note), and it's cuttable right now.
2. **Or do you want production guidance too**, as a cheap second pace, because the 409 that bit you was confusing enough to be worth sharpening?

If you can answer those, I think it's time to cut. If you're still unsure whether the probe's 403-owned-elsewhere reading is reliable enough to build on, that's the one thing I'd want to pin down first — want me to dig into whether the GET-against-candidate-projectId actually returns a clean 403 vs a 404 for cross-identity reserved IDs before we commit a pace to it?

What's your instinct on the fixture-vs-both question?

content.text:
Now I have the complete file. Let me analyze the depot-moniker auto-picker mechanism:

## Report: Depot-Moniker Auto-Picker Analysis

### 1. **Core Functions for Moniker Derivation/Picking**

**Primary function:** `rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker` (lines 336-381)
- **Signature:** `fn rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker(list_result: &rbtdri_InvokeResult, root: &Path, family_stem: &str) -> Result<String, String>`
- **What it does step-by-step:**
  1. Resolves the cloud-prefix subdir from rbrd.env (line 341)
  2. Constructs path to the BURV output directory under the cloud_prefix subdir (lines 342-345)
  3. Reads all filesystem entries in that directory (lines 346-351)
  4. Walks entries looking for `.depot` fact files matching the pattern `<family_stem>NNNNNN.depot` (lines 352-374)
  5. Extracts and validates the numeric suffix (6 digits, lines 367-373)
  6. Tracks the maximum suffix found (line 374)
  7. Returns next moniker as `<family_stem><max+1>` or floor if none exist (lines 376-381)

**Secondary helpers:**
- `rbtdrk_family_stem(tincture: &str) -> String` (lines 135-137): composes `RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE + tincture` → `"canest3" + tincture`
- `rbtdrk_cloud_prefix_subdir(root: &Path) -> Result<String, String>` (lines 317-322): strips trailing `-` from RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX to get subdir name
- `rbtdrk_compose_project_id(root: &Path, moniker: &str) -> Result<String, String>` (lines 306-311): derives projectId as `<cloud_prefix>d-<moniker>`

---

### 2. **"Next Free" Search Mechanism**

**Search method:**
- **NOT** a GCP API call (like `depot_list` / `projects:search`)
- **IS** a **filesystem walk of BURV fact-file output** after invoking `depot_list`
- Caller **must** invoke `depot_list` fresh in the current process (lines 471-487)
- The fact-file scan itself IS the collision check (line 333-334)

**How it increments:**
- Parses all matching filenames: `<family_stem>NNNNNN.depot` where NNNNNN is exactly 6 decimal digits (lines 363-373)
- Finds maximum numeric suffix across all files (line 374)
- Next moniker = `family_stem + (max + 1)` or floor if no files exist (lines 376-381)

---

### 3. **Moniker/ProjectId Format and Family-Stem/Floor Logic**

**Format examples:**
- Family stem base: `"canest3"` (line 85) — era-bumped from `canest2` and prior to avoid reserved projectId collisions (lines 82-84)
- Composed with tincture: `"canest3" + "bhm"` → `"canest3bhm"`
- Full moniker: `"canest3bhm100000"` (lines 85, 92-93)
- ProjectId: `"cancd-canest3bhm100000"` (derived from cloud_prefix `"canc-"` + moniker)

**Numeric suffix computation:**
- **Floor value:** `RBTDRK_FAMILY_NUMERIC_FLOOR = 100000` (u32, line 92)
- **Width:** `RBTDRK_FAMILY_NUMERIC_WIDTH = 6` characters (line 93)
- **Increment:** simple `max + 1` (line 377)
- **Format:** zero-padded u32 parsed/formatted as 6-digit decimal string (lines 367-373)

**Example timeline for one station:**
- Run 1: `canest3bhm100000`
- Run 2: `canest3bhm100001`
- Run 3: `canest3bhm100002`
(Each run picks the next integer suffix by walking fact files)

---

### 4. **Availability Probe**

**Current state:** NO explicit GET/availability probe against a candidate projectId.

**What exists:**
- Filesystem collision check only: scans fact files from the current `depot_list` invocation (line 334)
- Post-levy **validation** (lines 542-575): reads the fact-file output and cross-checks derived projectId against the fact-file value
  - Quote (lines 569-575):
    ```rust
    if composed != fact_project_id {
        return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
            "project_id mismatch: RBDC compose='{}' vs depot-list fact='{}' \
             (RBDC kindle derivation diverged from payor creation)",
            composed, fact_project_id
        ));
    }
    ```
  - This detects if the payor didn't create the projectId we expected, but does NOT probe GCP directly for existence before levy

---

### 5. **Error Handling for Project-Creation 409s**

**Current state:** No explicit 409 handling.

**What happens:**
- Lines 502-518: `rbtdrk_invoke_logged` calls `RBTDGC_LEVY_DEPOT` (levy command)
- If `levy.exit_code != 0`, fail with stderr dump (lines 513-517)
- No 409-specific logic or collision-resolution retry loop
- Quote (lines 513-517):
  ```rust
  if levy.exit_code != 0 {
      return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
          "depot levy exit {}\n{}",
          levy.exit_code, levy.stderr
      ));
  }
  ```

---

### **Critical Insight for Your Fix Design**

The **vulnerability** is in the collision-safety contract (lines 333-335):
```
Caller contract: `list_result` MUST be from a freshly-invoked depot_list
that ran in the current process. The fact-file scan IS the collision
check — stale state means picking a colliding moniker. Do not reuse a
`list_result` across cases or pass an operator-cached value.
```

The allocator **relies entirely on facts emitted by the active payor's own `depot_list` invocation**. It has **no visibility into globally-reserved projectIds** (like deleted `canest2bhm100000` held in DELETE_REQUESTED state). The fix surface is:
1. Either probe GCP directly before levy (GET projects API)
2. Or trap 409 from levy, increment, and retry
3. Or enlarge the family-stem bumping window to escape the reservation window (~30 days per line 82)


### 5. **Error Handling for Project-Creation 409s**

**Current state:** No explicit 409 handling.

**What happens:**
- Lines 502-518: `rbtdrk_invoke_logged` calls `RBTDGC_LEVY_DEPOT` (levy command)
- If `levy.exit_code != 0`, fail with stderr dump (lines 513-517)
- No 409-specific logic or collision-resolution retry loop
- Quote (lines 513-517):
  ```rust

      return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
          "depot levy exit {}\n{}",
          levy.exit_code, levy.stderr
      ));
  }
  ```

The **vulnerability** is in the collision-safety contract (lines 333-335):
```
Caller contract: `list_result` MUST be from a freshly-invoked depot_list
that ran in the current process. The fact-file scan IS the collision
check — stale state means picking a colliding moniker. Do not reuse a

```

The allocator **relies entirely on facts emitted by the active payor's own `depot_list` invocation**. It has **no visibility into globally-reserved projectIds** (like deleted `canest2bhm100000` held in DELETE_REQUESTED state). The fix surface is:
1. Either probe GCP directly before levy (GET projects API)
2. Or trap 409 from levy, increment, and retry
3. Or enlarge the family-stem bumping window to escape the reservation window (~30 days per line 82)

Oriented. I read both surfaces (parallel) and the picture is sharper — and a bit different from my opening musing in one important way.

## What's actually in the code

**Production levy — `rbgp_depot_levy` (`Tools/rbk/rbgp_Payor.sh:1014`)**
- Confirmed: **no auto-pick.** It bakes `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` into `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` and POSTs `projects:create`.
- A 409 flows through `rbgu_http_require_ok` → `buc_die` with the API's error text. Already loud.
- Operator guidance **already exists**: `rbgp_depot_unmake:1330` says *"Recovery: rename RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER in rbrd.env, or run rbw-MZ…"* and the levy preamble (line 1007) tells you to set the moniker first.
- So the "production = clearer 409 + guidance" option is even lighter than I pitched — most of the guidance is there. It'd be a one-line message sharpening, at most.

**Fixture picker — `rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs:336`)**
- Two corrections to my mental model:
  1. It doesn't even hit a GCP search at the pick step — it **walks local `.depot` fact files** emitted by the current-process `depot_list` run, takes `max(suffix)+1` off a floor of `100000`. The GCP `projects:search` is one layer back, in `depot_list`. So cross-identity IDs are invisible *twice over*.
  2. **The family-stem bump is already landed.** `RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE = "canest3"` (line 85), with a comment that it was era-bumped from `canest2` *specifically to escape reserved-projectId collisions* (~30-day window). The "workaround" the paddock describes in past tense is the **current live mechanism**.

There is **no availability probe anywhere** — production or fixture. So option B isn't "swap proxy for probe," it's "add a GCP API call the fixture has never made."

## How this refines the muse

The decision is more lopsided than I first said, because `canest3` already bought us out of the immediate collision. That reframes pace-cutting into three genuinely distinct postures:

- **A — Mitigated, defer the robustness.** The stem bump already escaped the window. Cut one doc pace: record "stem-bump-as-escape-hatch" in `RBSDN`/`RBSDE` and the paddock, wrap the heat. Honest, cheap, matches the "low urgency" framing.
- **B — Real self-heal (the fixture probe).** Add a GET-against-candidate-`projectId` to `rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker` so auto-pick increments past cross-identity-reserved IDs without ever needing a manual stem bump. One impl pace + spec note. This is the only option that removes the manual-bump ritual.
- **C — Production message polish.** Sharpen the levy 409 to name the cross-identity-reservation cause. Tiny, independent, can ride alongside A or B.

The technical risk I flagged earlier still gates **B specifically**, and it's now more pointed: since the fixture makes *no* such GET today, B rests entirely on unverified API behavior — does `GET projects/{cross-identity-reserved-id}` return a clean `403` (owned-elsewhere) vs `404` (free)? If that's mushy, the probe can't distinguish "take it" from "skip it," and B collapses back toward A.

My honest read: given `canest3` already holds, this smells like an **A + C heat** (document the escape hatch, sharpen the message), with **B held as an itch** unless you actually expect frequent payor-identity switches. But that hinges on a value judgment I don't have — **how often do you foresee switching payor identity?** That single answer is what tips A-vs-B. Want me to settle the 403/404 question empirically first so B is real-or-dead before you decide?

content.text:
Of course. Let me back all the way up and tell the story in plain terms.

## The system

Recipe Bottle builds container images in the cloud. To do that, each "depot" is its own **GCP project** — a sandbox with its own build infrastructure, service accounts, registries. When you provision a new depot, RBK creates a fresh GCP project for it.

Every GCP project needs a **project ID**, and here's the catch that started all this:

- Project IDs are **globally unique across all of Google Cloud** — not just yours. If *anyone* on Earth has a project called `foo`, you can't make one called `foo`.
- When you delete a project, the ID stays **reserved for ~30 days** (the project sits in `DELETE_REQUESTED` limbo). You can't reuse it, and neither can anyone else.

So RBK names depots with a counter: `canest2bhm100000`, `canest2bhm100001`, and so on. To pick the next one, it asks Google "what projects do I already have?" and increments past the highest.

## What broke

That "what do I already have?" question is **scoped to your own identity**. It only sees *your* projects — including your own deleted-but-still-reserved ones. That's fine for normal use: if you churn through depots, the search sees your pending-deletes and skips past them. **Self-healing.**

The blind spot is **someone else's** reserved IDs. Their deleted project's ID is globally taken but invisible to your search.

This actually bit the project: the payor (billing identity) switched from a **gmail account** (no org) to the **scaleinvariant** account. The gmail identity had deleted `canest2bhm100000`, so that ID was globally reserved — but the scaleinvariant identity's search couldn't see it. So the allocator kept confidently deriving `canest2bhm100000`, and project creation kept failing with a **409 "already exists"** that looked inexplicable, because from scaleinvariant's view that name was free.

## What was done about it (already, last time)

Someone bumped the name family from `canest2` → `canest3`. New depots now start `canest3bhm100000` — a clean namespace nobody's deleted projects are squatting on. **The immediate pain is gone.** That bump is live in the code right now.

## What this heat is actually for

So ₣BR isn't fixing a live fire — the fire's out. It's asking: **do we want a permanent fix, or was the namespace bump good enough?**

The "permanent fix" idea is an **availability probe**: before picking a name, actually ask Google "is *this specific ID* free?" (a direct GET on the candidate, rather than the identity-scoped "what do I own?" proxy). That would self-heal across identity switches too — no more manual namespace bumps when you change billing accounts.

The heat is **stabled with no paces cut** because two things were never decided:
1. **Where** — fix the test fixture's auto-picker (where the silent collision lives), or the production levy (which already fails loudly)?
2. **What** — build the real probe, or just make the error message clearer and call it documented?

That's the whole shape. It's low-urgency robustness work — the namespace bump bought time, and the question is whether the permanent fix is worth building now or should become an itch.

message.content: Ah a distant memory is occurring: is this heat really about improving the 'autoincrement' strategy to go off of what is publically visible rather than what is accessible from the API space?

content.text:
Yes — that's the heart of it, and you've put your finger on exactly the right axis. Let me sharpen the framing just slightly, because the distinction isn't quite "public vs. API" — both are API calls. It's *which question you ask the API*:

**Current strategy — "what do I own?"**
The allocator increments off an **identity-scoped** view: `projects:search` returns *your* projects. That's a **proxy** for the real constraint. It happens to work as long as the only colliders are your own deleted projects, because those show up in your own search. It's the convenient question, not the correct one.

**The strategy this heat contemplates — "does this ID exist *at all*?"**
A direct `GET projects/{candidate-id}`. The answer doesn't depend on who owns it — it tests the **actual global constraint**, which is the thing that genuinely governs whether creation succeeds. A reserved ID owned by *anyone* responds; an unreserved one doesn't.

So your distant memory is right: **the heat is fundamentally about moving the autoincrement off an identity-scoped proxy and onto the real global namespace.** Everything else in the paddock (fixture-vs-production, probe-vs-guidance) is downstream of that one idea.

The one honest caveat — and it's the thing that determines whether the idea is even buildable: a `GET` on a globally-reserved-but-not-yours ID has to come back as a **clean, distinguishable signal**. The hope is:

- `404` → free, take it
- `403` → exists/reserved elsewhere, increment past it
- `200` → yours, increment past it

If Google instead collapses "reserved by someone else" into a `404` (to avoid leaking that the ID exists), then the global probe **can't see the very collisions it was built to catch** — and the whole strategy folds back into the namespace-bump approach you already have. That 403-vs-404 behavior is the load-bearing unknown.

So the real question this heat turns on: **is the global namespace actually probe-able from your identity, or does Google hide cross-identity existence?** Want me to settle that empirically — it's a single GET against a known-reserved-elsewhere ID — before we decide whether the strategy is real or a dead end?

content.text:
Conclusive, on both API surfaces. Free and stranger-taken are **byte-for-byte indistinguishable** — identical `403 PERMISSION_DENIED`, identical message, v1 and v3 alike. This is Google's deliberate **anti-enumeration** behavior: it refuses to confirm or deny existence of a resource you can't access, precisely so nobody can probe the global namespace for taken IDs.

## What this does to the heat

The load-bearing unknown I flagged has resolved **against the probe** — and it resolves against the heat's *founding premise*. Your distant memory was "move the autoincrement onto what's globally visible." The experiment says: **nothing is globally visible.** There is no read that distinguishes "free" from "taken by someone else." The identity-scoped search isn't a lazy proxy we can upgrade — it's the *only* read Google will give you.

So the probe strategy (option B) is dead on arrival. That's a real result, not a setback — it eliminates the option that was eating the most design oxygen.

## What's actually left

With reads ruled out, the only oracle for global availability is the **write**: attempt to create, and a `409` tells you the ID is taken (by anyone). That collapses the real fix space to:

- **B′ — create-and-retry-on-409.** Loop the *project-create step* (the cheap first step of levy, before any worker-pool/SA provisioning), incrementing the moniker on 409 until it succeeds. The 409 is the probe. This is identity-independent and self-heals across payor switches — the thing the dead probe was supposed to do, achieved the only way Google permits. Failed attempts cost nothing (a non-created ID isn't reserved); you only "burn" the ID you keep.
- **A — namespace bump.** Already landed (`canest3`). Manual escape hatch.
- **C — clearer 409 guidance.** Tiny.

Note B′ has a structural cost B didn't: it **couples picking to creating.** Today the fixture picks (`rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker`, off local fact files) and the bash levy creates, as separate steps. A retry loop has to wrap create and feed back into pick — a more invasive change than dropping in a read-probe would have been.

## Where I'd land

One thing is unambiguous regardless of what you decide: **this empirical finding has to go into the paddock.** Right now the paddock's "Undecided" section invites a future agent to go build the read-probe — straight into this wall. Recording "Google hides cross-identity existence; reads can't distinguish free from taken; the write is the only oracle" is the single highest-value action here, and it's the kind of hard-won fact the paddock exists to hold.

After that, the genuine decision narrows to: **is create-retry-on-409 (B′) worth the pick/create coupling, or does the `canest3` bump + a sharper error message close this out as "mitigated"?** That's now a clean either/or, and it's yours to call.

Want me to write the finding into the paddock first, and then we decide B′-vs-mitigate with the dead end properly buried?

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding GCP project ID availability and the 409 error:

## Project ID Uniqueness and Availability

A project ID cannot be in use or previously used; this includes deleted projects. This is important because projects that users have soft-deleted count against their quota, and these projects are fully deleted after 30 days.

## 409 Error and "alreadyExists"

The search results show several examples where users encounter the 409 "alreadyExists" error when trying to create GCP resources. When creating a project, the error message "Error 409: Requested entity already exists, alreadyExists" indicates the project ID is unavailable.

## Key Constraints for Project IDs

A project ID must start with a lowercase letter, and can contain only ASCII letters, digits, and hyphens, and must be between 6 and 30 characters.

The important takeaway is that even deleted projects remain reserved for 30 days and prevent reuse of the same project ID, which is why the 409 error occurs when attempting to create a project with a previously used ID during this 30-day grace period.

This is a known behavior in Google Cloud Platform's API design. The specific phenomenon you're asking about—where `resourcemanager.projects.get` returns `PERMISSION_DENIED` instead of `NOT_FOUND` for nonexistent projects—appears to be an intentional design choice in GCP's security model.

The reasoning behind this behavior relates to information security: GCP error messages contain: "Error 403: The caller does not have permission, or the resource may not exist." This ambiguous response is intentional because revealing whether a resource exists or not could leak information to unauthorized users. By returning `PERMISSION_DENIED` even when a project doesn't exist, GCP prevents users without proper permissions from discovering information about the existence or non-existence of projects they shouldn't have access to.

This means that when you receive a `PERMISSION_DENIED` error from the `resourcemanager.projects.get` API call, you should verify:
1. Whether you have the necessary IAM permissions (`resourcemanager.projects.get`)
2. Whether the Cloud Resource Manager API is enabled
3. Whether organization policies might be blocking access

Rather than assuming the project doesn't exist, you should first confirm you have the appropriate permissions and access to the resource.

content.text:
Both pillars confirmed externally — my empirical result isn't a local quirk.

## What the web confirms

**1. The 403-not-404 is intentional, documented GCP behavior.** Google deliberately returns `403 "The caller does not have permission, or the resource may not exist"` rather than distinguishing the two — precisely *to avoid leaking whether a resource exists* to callers who can't access it. That's exactly what I measured: free and stranger-taken IDs collapse to identical `PERMISSION_DENIED`. So there is no read-side oracle, by design, and that's not going to change.

**2. The collision model is exactly as the paddock describes.** Google's own docs: a project ID "cannot be in use or previously used; this includes deleted projects" — soft-deleted projects "count against quota" and stay reserved until "fully deleted after 30 days." Create returns `409 Requested entity already exists` on any taken ID, yours or not. The 409 is reliable, documented, and identity-independent.

## The verdict, now with confidence from three sources

| Source | Says |
|--------|------|
| My CRM v3/v1 probe (live) | free ≡ taken-elsewhere ≡ `403 PERMISSION_DENIED`, byte-identical |
| GCP error-design docs | 403-ambiguity is intentional anti-enumeration |
| GCP project docs | IDs globally unique, 30-day reservation, create→`409` is the signal |

**The heat's founding premise — "autoincrement off what's globally visible" — is not achievable.** Nothing is globally visible; Google designed it that way on purpose. The only oracle for global availability is the **write**, and the `409` is that oracle: reliable, documented, identity-independent.

That leaves the fix space exactly where my prior turn landed it, now with no remaining uncertainty:
- **B′ — create-and-retry-on-409** is the *only* robust self-heal (the write is the probe), at the cost of coupling pick→create.
- **A (canest3 bump, done) + C (sharper 409 message)** = "mitigated, robustness deferred."

The empirical dead-end is now durable knowledge worth burying in the paddock so no future agent re-chases the read-probe. **Shall I write the finding into the paddock** — the 403 anti-enumeration result, the three-source confirmation, and the reframed B′-vs-mitigate choice — and then you make the call on whether to cut a B′ pace or close this as mitigated?

Sources:
- [Access control for projects with IAM — Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/access-control-proj)
- [GCP 403 PERMISSION_DENIED troubleshooting — CloudWebSchool](https://cloudwebschool.com/docs/gcp/troubleshooting/permission-denied-errors/)
- [Create projects — Resource Manager, Google Cloud](https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-projects)
- [error creating project: Error 409: Requested entity already exists — terraform-provider-google #10267](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-provider-google/issues/10267)

# Scars

Closed ideas with lessons learned.

## rbgp-create-governor (2025-12-27)
**Status**: Superseded by heat `b251227-governor-reset-operation`

Original itch called for `rbgp_create_governor()` following RBAGS spec. During heat planning, we discovered:
- The spec assumed pristine state (no existing Governor)
- Real-world needs reset semantics for credential rotation
- Naming convention needed standardization (governor-{timestamp})

**Outcome**: Implemented `rbgp_governor_reset()` instead - idempotent create-or-replace operation that deletes existing governor-* SAs before creating a new one.

**Lesson**: Implementation itches should note whether pristine-state or idempotent semantics are intended.

**Lesson**: Implementation itches should note whether pristine-state or idempotent semantics are intended.

## depot-projectid-collision-global-probe (2026-05-30)
**Status**: Investigated and closed without code change (heat `rbk-16-gcp-hardening` archived).

Cross-identity depot projectId reservation collisions: GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after deletion (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker autoincrement picks "next free" off an *identity-scoped* view (`projects:search` / local fact files), so it is blind to IDs reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project. This bit the project on a payor switch (gmail → scaleinvariant): gmail's deleted `cancbhm-d-canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to scaleinvariant's allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor and 409'ing.

**The tempting fix is impossible.** "Move the autoincrement onto what's globally visible" cannot work — there is no global read oracle. Verified live (CRM v3 *and* v1 `GET /projects/{id}`): a free ID and a stranger-owned ID return **byte-identical `403 PERMISSION_DENIED`**. GCP does this deliberately (anti-enumeration — it won't confirm existence to a caller who can't access the resource; the error text is literally "the caller does not have permission, or the resource may not exist"). Confirmed by Google docs. So reads cannot distinguish free from taken-elsewhere.

**Outcome**: No change. The live case was already mitigated by a family-stem bump (`canest2` → `canest3`, see `RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE` in `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`), which moved into a clean namespace. Urgency is low — recurs only on payor-identity switch.

**Lesson**: The *only* oracle for global projectId availability is the **write** — attempt create, catch `409 alreadyExists`. If this ever recurs and is worth fixing, the sole robust path is create-and-retry-on-409 (increment the moniker on 409 until create succeeds; failed creates reserve nothing), at the cost of coupling moniker-pick to project-create (today they are separate: pick in `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, create in the `rbgp_depot_levy` bash). Do **not** re-attempt a read-side availability probe — it is provably impossible.

## depot-projectid-collision-global-probe (2026-05-30)
**Status**: Investigated and closed without code change (heat `rbk-16-gcp-hardening` archived).

Cross-identity depot projectId reservation collisions: GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after deletion (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker autoincrement picks "next free" off an *identity-scoped* view (`projects:search` / local fact files), so it is blind to IDs reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project. This bit the project on a payor switch (gmail → scaleinvariant): gmail's deleted `cancbhm-d-canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to scaleinvariant's allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor and 409'ing.

**The tempting fix is impossible.** "Move the autoincrement onto what's globally visible" cannot work — there is no global read oracle. Verified live (CRM v3 *and* v1 `GET /projects/{id}`): a free ID and a stranger-owned ID return **byte-identical `403 PERMISSION_DENIED`**. GCP does this deliberately (anti-enumeration — it won't confirm existence to a caller who can't access the resource; the error text is literally "the caller does not have permission, or the resource may not exist"). Confirmed by Google docs. So reads cannot distinguish free from taken-elsewhere.

**Outcome**: No change. The live case was already mitigated by a family-stem bump (`canest2` → `canest3`, see `RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE` in `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`), which moved into a clean namespace. Urgency is low — recurs only on payor-identity switch.

**Lesson**: The *only* oracle for global projectId availability is the **write** — attempt create, catch `409 alreadyExists`. If this ever recurs and is worth fixing, the sole robust path is create-and-retry-on-409 (increment the moniker on 409 until create succeeds; failed creates reserve nothing), at the cost of coupling moniker-pick to project-create (today they are separate: pick in `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, create in the `rbgp_depot_levy` bash). Do **not** re-attempt a read-side availability probe — it is provably impossible.

**Status**: Superseded by heat `b251227-governor-reset-operation`

**Scar recorded** — `jjz_scar.md` → `depot-projectid-collision-global-probe (2026-05-30)`, committed `12ec8b9f9`.
